content.claim: Poland delayed tracking from age 15 to age 16 in 1999 reform; subsequent evidence documented improved PISA outcomes and labour-market gains (3 percentage point employment gain, 4% earnings gain), but the reform was substantially reversed after 2016, indicating low institutional durability despite measured success.
content.context: Fresh live research through the International comparison lens
limitations: Attribution of outcomes to tracking delay alone is confounded with other concurrent reforms
Polish institutional and political context differs from Hungary (centralization, ethnic composition, historical legacy)
Reversal explanation not fully documented - motivations for rollback not specified in notes
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
